content:
Using a layer appears to work great for determining when to redraw.
Pushed a new version which should address all comments.

Ready for a full review.
This seems pretty solid. Only a few more tweaks. :-)

Given how expensive this approach is, we should make sure that this widget cannot run outside of debug mode. Can you add some code that will throw an exception in profile and release builds, saying that this widget isn't available outside debug mode? Also, please add a detailed comment to the Layer subclass explaining that this approach is horrifically for performance and is only used here because this is limited to debug mode and that nobody should think of using this in production code. :-)

I'm not sure whether it is feasible, but here's a design that might be more efficient:

1. Move the widget tree that is under inspection to an off-screen pipeline owner.
2. When the off-screen pipeline owner requests a visual update, request a visual update in the on-screen pipeline owner.
3. In a persistent frame callback, flush the off-screen pipeline owner.
4. Read the relevant information from the off-screen pipeline owner and generate writes into a normal on-screen render tree.
5. Let the on-screen pipeline owner flush the on-screen render tree as usual.

This approach will let the inspector use all the machinery of the whole system to work efficiently instead of attempting to do its own layout and paint phase during the addToScene walk of the composite phase.

Essentially, using a separate pipeline owner separates the work from the inspector's pipeline and the tree under inspection so that they can run in series instead of in parallel.

If it is too disruptive to move the tree that is under inspection off-screen, another approach would be for the inspector to build a render tree in an off-screen pipeline owner and then flush the inspector's pipeline during the composite phase.  This approach is less elegant in the sense that we'd still be running a mini version of the whole system inside the composite phase of the real system, but at least you'd have all the machinery of the whole system available to you so you wouldn't need to do grossly inefficient things like re-creating TextPainter objects for every animation frame.

Please don't take my comments as blocking in any way.  It's just an idea for an approach that might be more scalable (assuming it works at all).

> the UI I need to render in the overlay is extremely simple

These tools tend to get more sophisticated over time.  There's some value in choosing an approach with a solid foundation to be ready for the feature to grow over time.
@jacob314 Looks like you didn't push your recent changes (or at least, the first comment, which you say is done, doesn't seem to have been done).
Fixed the typo in the git push command. Ready for review now.

Removed the direct dependency on material by moving the tooltip code to /rendering and injecting a builder to create the button to re-enter inspect mode. If no builder is specified, the inspector always stays in select mode which is fine as toggling back and forth between select and view modes is an experiment which we could easily remove.

Note that the hit testing code was also simplified to eliminate all dependence on RenderBox hit testing as RenderBox hit testing started giving undesirable results for sample applications due to an added gesture recognizer. The new heuristic is the render object with the smaller bounding rectangle wins if multiple hit test match occurs and the match is not on the edge of one of the rectangles.
Are there any tests for the inspector itself?
Added tests to the inspector. Made  _WidgetInspectorState and _InspectorSelection public for easier testing.
Ready for next round of review. Add tests for the inspector itself, addressed all other outstanding comments.

Modulo the minor comments:
![LGTM](http://junkyard.damowmow.com/579)

## [flutter_tools] Add ARM64 Linux host and cross-building option support

## 1. Description

I want to support following functions.

* The Flutter SDK works on Arm64 Linux Host (e.g. `flutter run -d linux`, `flutter doctor`)
* Flutter apps can be built on Arm64 Linux Host.
* Flutter apps can be **cross-build** on **x64** Linux Host for Arm64 target devices.

### Design documents

https://docs.google.com/document/d/19tzWySgtgtTA99XQsjx5Pg0SFJeZKXyUlYavR0EXv8c/edit?usp=sharing

### Use Case

#### Running the flutter app on Arm64 Linux Host.

```
$ flutter doctor
$ flutter run -d linux
```

![Screenshot from 2020-08-05 12-52-42](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/62131389/89372186-729c6980-d720-11ea-861d-1de1cbc57e08.png)

#### Cross-building a Flutter app for Arm64 on x64 Linux Host.

If you want to cross-build for Arm64, you need to prepare a target sysroot. You can specify it as a command line argument.

```
$ flutter build linux --target-platform=linux-arm64 --target-sysroot=/target-sysroot-path
```

## 2. Related PRs

 - [x] https://github.com/flutter/buildroot/pull/390
 - [x] https://github.com/flutter/engine/pull/20254
 - [x] https://github.com/flutter/engine/pull/22284

## 3. Related Issues

* https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/56992
* https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/60678

## 4. Tests

I have tested that it can be built and run for x84_64 Host and built for aarch64 to work with [Jeston Nano](https://developer.nvidia.com/embedded/jetson-nano-developer-kit).

## 5. Checklist

Before you create this PR confirm that it meets all requirements listed below by checking the relevant checkboxes (`[x]`). This will ensure a smooth and quick review process.

- [x] I read the [Contributor Guide] and followed the process outlined there for submitting PRs.
- [x] I signed the [CLA].
- [x] I read and followed the [Flutter Style Guide], including [Features we expect every widget to implement].
- [x] I read the [Tree Hygiene] wiki page, which explains my responsibilities.
- [x] I updated/added relevant documentation (doc comments with `///`).
- [x] All existing and new tests are passing.
- [x] The analyzer (`flutter analyze --flutter-repo`) does not report any problems on my PR.
- [x] I am willing to follow-up on review comments in a timely manner.

## Breaking Change

Did any tests fail when you ran them? Please read [Handling breaking changes].

- [ ] No, no existing tests failed, so this is *not* a breaking change.
- [x] Yes, this is a breaking change. *If not, delete the remainder of this section.*
   - [x] I wrote a design doc: [/go/flutter-support-multi-architecture](https://docs.google.com/document/d/19tzWySgtgtTA99XQsjx5Pg0SFJeZKXyUlYavR0EXv8c/edit?usp=sharing)
   - [ ] I got input from the developer relations team, specifically from: *Replace with the names of who gave advice*
   - [ ] I wrote a migration guide: https://flutter.dev/go/breaking-changes-template *Replace this with a link to a pull request that adds the migration guide to https://flutter.dev/docs/release/breaking-changes*

<!-- Links -->
[issue database]: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues
[Contributor Guide]: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Tree-hygiene#overview
[Test Coverage]: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Test-coverage-for-package%3Aflutter
[Flutter Style Guide]: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Style-guide-for-Flutter-repo
[Features we expect every widget to implement]: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Style-guide-for-Flutter-repo#features-we-expect-every-widget-to-implement
[CLA]: https://cla.developers.google.com/
[Tree Hygiene]: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Tree-hygiene
[Handling breaking changes]: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Tree-hygiene#handling-breaking-changes

I can point you in the right direction, but I don't really have bandwidth to work on this at the moment.

@christopherfujino

> did you actually build the binaries and get this working, or is this a proof of concept?

Yes, I did. I use my customized sysrootfs for aarch64, but here is a binary for sample flutter app built with Flutter SDK with this patch. Works with ARM64 platforms such as JetsonNao. Of course, I also use a locally built version of Flutter Engine.
https://github.com/HidenoriMatsubayashi/flutter-examples/blob/master/flutter_aarch64_sample.zip

![Screenshot from 2020-06-11 17-32-47](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/62131389/87402030-ee116c00-c5f5-11ea-9cce-29d3e9f2e7d2.png)

> I misunderstood and thought you had a working linux arm host setup.

I see. No problem at all.
By the way, I've also tried it partially. I did dart-sdk cross build for aarch64.

@HidenoriMatsubayashi That's awesome to hear!

Did you cross-build Dart directly from the https://github.com/dart-lang/sdk repo, or via Flutter's engine environment? I was able to do the former, but got stuck on the latter before I had to move on to other things.

@christopherfujino 

> Did you cross-build Dart directly from the https://github.com/dart-lang/sdk repo, or via Flutter's engine environment?

A while ago, I tried to cross-build Dart directly from the https://github.com/dart-lang/sdk repo for running Flutter SDK (flutter commands) on ARM64 Host. I basically tried the steps [here](https://github.com/dart-lang/sdk/wiki/Building).  I think the build result worked on Raspberry Pi 4.
(By the way, I use Flutter Engine environment for building Flutter Engine (so files etc.).)

> I was able to do the former, but got stuck on the latter before I had to move on to other things.

What exactly was the problem? If you want to support running flutter sdk on ARM64 Host, I may be able to help you..
Any updates on this?

Addressing some of the questions/comments in the initial description:

> Should the result of the x86_64 and aarch64 builds be in separate directories?

Yes, definitely (unless there's a fat/universal build option for Linux that I'm not aware of). We should not stomp builds with different target outputs.

> Should we include aarch64 rootfs in the Flutter SDK?

This seems out of scope for Flutter; we don't ship and use a sysroot for the current builds.

> We will need infra updates so that you can download artifacts (Flutter Eingine build results, etc.) to the following locations.

This would be the first place to start, IMO. We also need confirmation/commitment from Dart folks that cross-compile tooling (e.g., for gen_snapshot) is a supported configuration on Linux.

@nikiask 
I'm a bit busy. But I'm still working.

@stuartmorgan 
Thank you for your reply.

@christopherfujino 
FYI. Here is the procedure for cross-build and running dart-sdk I tried. Basically cross-build procedure is as per [the official wiki](https://github.com/dart-lang/sdk/wiki/Building-Dart-SDK-for-ARM-processors).

1. Build dart-sdk on Host PC (Ubuntu x64)
```
$ mkdir dart-sdk
$ cd dart-sdk
$ git clone https://chromium.googlesource.com/chromium/tools/depot_tools.git
$ export PATH="$PATH:$PWD/depot_tools"
$ fetch dart
$ cd dart
$ ./tools/build.py --no-goma -m release -a arm64
$ cd out/ReleaseXARM64/
$ tar zcf /tmp/dart-sdk.tar.gz dart-sdk
```

2. run Flutte SDK on Jetson Nano (Ubuntu arm64)
```
$ git clone https://github.com/flutter/flutter.git
$ export PATH=$PATH:./flutter/bin
$ tar xf dart-sdk.tar.gz
```

Replace dart-sdk after the error.
```
$ flutter doctor
  build error!!

$ rm -rf flutter/bin/cache/dart-sdk
$ cp -rf dart-sdk flutter/bin/cache
````

> @christopherfujino
> FYI. Here is the procedure for cross-build and running dart-sdk I tried. Basically cross-build procedure is as per [the official wiki](https://github.com/dart-lang/sdk/wiki/Building-Dart-SDK-for-ARM-processors).

Thanks for the response. Sorry I didn't respond, I've been busy putting out fires. I was also able to build the Dart SDK via the Dart repo directly. However, when I tried building it via the Flutter engine environment ([link](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Setting-up-the-Engine-development-environment), this is how the Dart SDK that the Flutter SDK uses gets built) I got missing headers errors. I'm guessing I need to provide a sysroot? @HidenoriMatsubayashi

@christopherfujino 

>  I'm guessing I need to provide a sysroot?

Yes. There are two ways. One is to use an external sysroot. Another way is to modify `buildroot` to use `debian_sid_arm64-sysroot`. I have tried both, and built Flutter Engine.

I could cross-build and pass the build.. I am going to send the PR.
https://github.com/HidenoriMatsubayashi/engine/tree/topic-support-linux-aarch64-cross-build
https://github.com/HidenoriMatsubayashi/buildroot/tree/topic-support-linux-aarch64-cross-build

`.gclient file` that I used is following.

```
solutions = [
  {
    "managed": False,
    "name": "src/flutter",
    "url": "https://github.com/HidenoriMatsubayashi/engine.git@9158ef84c326dc7260bfe39dd65189dd033d0b29",
    "custom_deps": {},
    "deps_file": "DEPS",
    "safesync_url": "",
  },
]
```

You need to modify `flutter/DEPS` file by yourself.
```
  'src': 'https://github.com/flutter/buildroot.git' + '@' + 'fe3b46e595e7ce1350e11aa0c90365976051f4a3',
↓
  'src': 'https://github.com/HidenoriMatsubayashi/buildroot.git' + '@' + '5658a5f9168a0025d25eb700b607abc4b1e247af',
```

Build Flutter Engine. The build is successful. But it is necessary to be careful about version combinations when actually run a Flutter app because of version check of DartVM.
```
$ ./flutter/tools/gn --target-os linux --linux-cpu arm64 --runtime-mode release
$ ninja -C out/linux_release_arm64

$ ./flutter/tools/gn --target-os linux --linux-cpu arm64 --runtime-mode debug
$ ninja -C out/linux_debug_arm64
```

I confirmed that the dart-sdk built above works on ARM64 Host. However, the flutter app has not been executed yet due to the error of dart-sdk. I am currently investigating..

```

sudo apt-get install lib32stdc++6 .  Unable to install, how do you solve this problem

I think there are enough questions as to what this should look like exactly for the developer, and enough points that will be in common with other platforms (e.g., eventual Windows arm64 support) that we should really have a design doc for the tooling plan before moving forward with a PR. It would need to answer questions like what the targets should be called and what they mean, e.g.:
- Is `linux` still a build option (vs linux-x64 and linux-arm64 or similar)? If so, does it mean "same as host" or "x64"?
- How should the build output be structured for multi-arch output?
- For Linux, how is the sysroot handled?

@stuartmorgan 

Thank you for your comment and advice.

> we should really have a design doc for the tooling plan before moving forward with a PR

I agree with you. Is it enough to add my idea to the description so that we can discuss? Or is there any other way?

> Is linux still a build option (vs linux-x64 and linux-arm64 or similar)? If so, does it mean "same as host" or "x64"?

No, it isn't. I'm sorry for confusing you. I updated the description. Now, I want to run the Flutter SDK on an arm64 host.

> How should the build output be structured for multi-arch output?

At the time of **self-build**, the build output will be output to `build/linux/`. It's the same as the current output directory structure. On the other hand, at the time of **cross-build**, it will be output to `build/linux-cross/`.

How about my idea?

> For Linux, how is the sysroot handled?

I have considered that an user can specify a sysroot prepared by themselves in the build option. I think it would be better to have an option for them to freely specify sysroot. Because Flutter Engine has similar options.

```
$ flutter build linux --target-platform=linux-arm64 --target-sysroot=/target-sysroot-path
```

But If the Flutter SDK download the sysroot used to cross-build the Flutter Engine first, they don't need to prepare anything.

> Is it enough to add my idea to the description so that we can discuss? Or is there any other way?

https://flutter.dev/docs/resources/design-docs

> 
> > Is linux still a build option (vs linux-x64 and linux-arm64 or similar)? If so, does it mean "same as host" or "x64"?
> 
> No, it isn't. I'm sorry for confusing you. I updated the description. Now, I want to run the Flutter SDK on an arm64 host.
> 
> > How should the build output be structured for multi-arch output?
> 
> At the time of **self-build**, the build output will be output to `build/linux/`. It's the same as the current output directory structure. On the other hand, at the time of **cross-build**, it will be output to `build/linux-cross/`.

Sorry, I wasn't clear. These weren't questions about how your patch works, these are questions that all stakeholders need to agree on what we *want* the solutions to be, not only for Linux, but for other platforms where we need this in the future such as Windows. (E.g., I'm not at all sold on having two different build directories whose contents depend on the host machine, vs., say, always doing linux/x64/... and linux/arm64/...)

And a design document is the best place to have a discussion like that.

I posted the design document. Please feel free to comment and review.
https://docs.google.com/document/d/19tzWySgtgtTA99XQsjx5Pg0SFJeZKXyUlYavR0EXv8c/edit?usp=sharing

https://flutter.dev/docs/resources/design-docs

Sorry, I misunderstood. By the way, I want to check the operation of Flutter for web on Raspberry Pi 4, but Chrome does not seem to support Arm64 Linux at the moment. But I can use Chromium on Raspberry Pi4, so does it work with Flutter for web? Please let me know if you know.
Yes it works. I use it on my development machine.

It can be sluggish but Flutter web works with Chromium on my arm devices. The easiest way to check is to open one of the examples or even to try with [dartpad](https://www.dartpad.dev/flutter).

Note: The sluggishness on my rk3399 devices with Flutter web is most likely because of the GPU driver and how it works with Chromium itself, not because of Flutter.

> Sorry, I misunderstood. By the way, I want to check the operation of Flutter for web on Raspberry Pi 4, but Chrome does not seem to support Arm64 Linux at the moment. But I can use Chromium on Raspberry Pi4, so does it work with Flutter for web? Please let me know if you know.

Chromium is definitely supported. I'm not sure the exact discovery rules, but I think there's also an env variable where you can explicitly specify the path.
